1: A type: "The king rejoices in your strength, LORD, and in your salvation how greatly he exults!" Psalm 21 is the companion to Psalm 20 — the thanksgiving after the battle for which Psalm 20 prayed. The king's rejoicing in divine strength after victory is the type of Christ's exultation after the resurrection. Hebrews 12:2 ("For the joy set before him he endured the cross, scorning its shame, and sat down at the right hand of the throne of God") speaks of the joy that follows the decisive battle — the same joy the king expresses in Psalm 21.
2: A type: "You have given him his heart's desire and have not withheld what his lips requested." The divine answer to the king's prayer — nothing withheld — is the type of Christ's prayer being answered in the resurrection. John 17:5 ("And now, Father, glorify me in your presence with the glory I had with you before the world began") is the request; the resurrection and ascension are the answer. The Father withholds nothing from the Son whose prayer is perfectly aligned with the Father's will.
3: A type: "For you meet him with blessings of goodness and set a crown of finest gold upon his head." The coronation blessing — the crown of fine gold placed on the king's head — is fulfilled in Christ's eschatological coronation. Revelation 14:14 (the Son of Man with "a golden crown on his head") and Hebrews 2:9 ("we see Jesus, who was made lower than the angels for a little while, now crowned with glory and honor because he suffered death") are the NT fulfillment of the golden crown of Psalms 21:3.
4: A direct connection: "He asked you for life, and you gave it to him — length of days forever and ever." The king's prayer for life answered by YHWH is the type of the resurrection. Christ asked for life (Hebrews 5:7: "he offered up prayers and petitions with fervent cries and tears to the one who could save him from death, and he was heard because of his reverent submission") and received it — not merely prolonged earthly life but "length of days forever and ever" (ʾōrek yāmîm ʿôlām wāʿed). The resurrection is the answer to this prayer: eternal life granted to the one who asked for life through death.
5: A type: "Through your salvation his glory is great; you have bestowed splendor and majesty upon him." The post-victory glorification of the king is the type of the post-resurrection glorification of Christ. Philippians 2:9-11: "Therefore God exalted him to the highest place and gave him the name that is above every name, that at the name of Jesus every knee should bow... and every tongue acknowledge that Jesus Christ is Lord." The "splendor and majesty (hôd wĕhādār)" bestowed on the king is the glory given to Christ after he "humbled himself by becoming obedient to death" (Philippians 2:8).
6: A direct connection: "For you make him most blessed forever; you fill him with gladness in your presence." Hebrews 12:2 captures this exactly: Christ endured the cross "for the joy set before him" and "sat down at the right hand of the throne of God." The "gladness in your presence (śimḥāh et-pānêkā)" is the eschatological joy that awaits the resurrection. John 17:24 ("Father, I want those you have given me to be with me where I am, and to see my glory that you have given me") shows Christ desiring to bring his community into this same gladness in the Father's presence.
7: A type: "For the king trusts in the LORD, and through the steadfast love of the Most High he will not be shaken." The king's trust in YHWH producing unshakeability is the type of Christ's perfect and unbroken trust in the Father — even through the passion. Isaiah 50:7-9 (Servant Song 3) shows the same unshakeable trust: "I have set my face like flint... He who vindicates me is near." The king who will not be shaken by opposition because of divine ḥesed is the type of the Messiah whose trust in the Father holds through crucifixion.
8: A shadow: "Your hand will reach all your enemies; your right hand will seize those who hate you." The divine conquest through the king's hand — all enemies seized — is the eschatological judgment imagery. 1 Corinthians 15:25-26: "he must reign until he has put all his enemies under his feet; the last enemy to be destroyed is death." The "hand reaching all enemies" of Psalm 21 is the ongoing campaign of Christ's reign that will not end until every enemy is subjected.
9: A shadow: "You will make them like a burning oven in the time of your anger; the LORD in his wrath will swallow them up, and fire will devour them." The fiery judgment of the king's enemies is the eschatological judgment imagery that the NT applies to Christ's final return. 2 Thessalonians 1:7-8: "He will punish those who do not know God and do not obey the gospel of our Lord Jesus. They will be punished with everlasting destruction." The burning oven of Psalm 21 and the "flaming fire" of 2 Thessalonians 1 both draw on the divine warrior's theophanic fire as judgment.
10: A shadow: "You will destroy their offspring from the earth and their descendants from among mankind." The complete end of the opposition's lineage — the enemies' dynasty ended — is the type of the final judgment's permanence. In the eschatological framework, those who oppose the Messiah have no "descendants" in the age to come — no continuation, no future, no inheritance. Revelation 20:10 ("they will be tormented day and night for ever and ever") is the ultimate end of the enemies' "lineage."
11: A shadow: "For they intended evil against you and devised a plot they cannot carry out." The enemies' evil intentions and failed schemes — they plotted but could not execute — is the type of Acts 4:27-28: "Indeed Herod and Pontius Pilate met together with the Gentiles and the people of Israel in this city to conspire against your holy servant Jesus... They did what your power and will had decided beforehand should happen." The enemy plot against the Messiah was real — and carried out — yet ultimately unable to succeed because the divine plan governed the outcome.
12: A shadow: "You will put them to flight as you aim your bow at their faces." The divine archer targeting the enemies who opposed the anointed king is the imagery of the divine warrior's eschatological judgment. Revelation 6:2 ("a rider on a white horse... given a crown, and he rode out as a conqueror bent on conquest") and Psalms 19:11-15 (Christ as the divine warrior) apply the divine-archer imagery of the Royal Psalms to the returning Christ whose conquest is final.
13: A direct connection: "Rise in your strength, O LORD! We will sing and praise your mighty deeds." The closing invocation — "Rise (rûmāh) in your strength!" — is the community's call for YHWH's exaltation after the royal victory. This becomes the Christological acclamation of the resurrection: "He has risen (ēgerthē)!" (Mark 16:6). The "rising" of YHWH in the king's victory is the type of the divine rising that is the resurrection of Christ — the definitive exaltation of YHWH in human history. "We will sing and praise your mighty deeds" is the response that the community of the risen Messiah enacts in all its worship.
